frameworks.name: flexible-stable synaptic coding
frameworks.one_line: stable synapses retain memory; flexible synapses update with new inputs

frameworks.name: neural network model of working memory
frameworks.one_line: RNN with mixed flexible/stable synapses simulates serial-position effects

frameworks.name: serial-position effect (primacy/recency)
frameworks.one_line: classical behavioural signature of sequential working memory

core_question: Do distinct stable and flexible neural codings jointly produce primacy and recency effects in sequential working memory?
key_assumptions: primacy and recency reflect dissociable encoding mechanisms
working memory uses a fixed resource that can be re-allocated across stable and flexible codes
human psychophysics constrains plausible synaptic-level models
manipulations: balance between flexible and stable synapses in the model
key_findings: stable encoding accounts for the primacy effect
flexible encoding accounts for the recency effect, supporting learning of new inputs
an RNN with both synapse types reproduces major serial-position signatures
pre-cued memory performance is modulated in a sequence-specific manner
interpretations: serial-position effects emerge spontaneously from the combination of stable and flexible codes
the flexible-stable balance controls resource allocation for sequence-specific memory performance
